coredata.dc:title: Improving anti-corrosion and mechanical properties of mild steel pipelines by using polyurea with nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Siddiqui, Zeeshan Ahmed
Wakeel, Aneela
Nasir, Muhammad Ali
Zubair, Muhammad
Ammar, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: ASTM A106 B grade is used to manufacture seamless steel pipe, which can be used at high temperature, pressure, and hard environmental conditions for oil and gas supplies but suffers from corrosion. Polyurea is an organic coating that is resistant to moisture, UV light, and chemicals and has a high range of service temperatures. In this study, the effect of polyurea coating with the addition of copper nanoparticles was investigated for improvement of anti-corrosion and mechanical behavior in 3.5 % NaCl solution for A106 steel. Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed to examine anticorrosion properties, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was used to examine quality coating, thickness, and distribution of copper nanoparticles. Wear, hardness, and tensile tests were performed to check mechanical properties. The corrosion rate decreases from 0.16264 mpy to 0.01376 mpy while coating resistance increases from 2.95 × 104 Ω cm2 to 2.474 × 105 Ω cm2. An increasing trend was observed during the tensile test for pure polyurea and pure polyurea with 2 % copper nanoparticles. i.e., 501 MPa and 536 MPa, respectively. Results showed pure polyurea and polyurea with copper nanoparticles on steel improve its anti-corrosion and mechanical properties significantly.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ASTM A106
Corrosion
Copper nanoparticles
Oil and gas pipelines
Polyurea

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tafel plot of S1 and S2 sample.
Nyquist plot. (a) S1 sample. (b) S2 sample.

SEM images at different zooming values. (a) SEM images of base metal, primer, and polyurea. (b) SEM image of curvy shape sample and average thickness value of polyurea. (c) SEM image of zinc-rich epoxy primer. (d) SEM image of pure polyurea. (e) SEM image of 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles in polyurea.
SEM image of 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles in polyurea.
Composition of ASTM A106 grade B.
Results of pin on disc apparatus.

head.ce:title: Improving anti-corrosion and mechanical properties of mild steel pipelines by using polyurea with nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Engineering Department, University of Engineering and Technology Taxila, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Engineering and Technology Taxila

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aMechanical Engineering Department, University of Engineering and Technology Taxila, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical & Nuclear Engineering, University of Sharjah, 27272, Sharjah, UAE
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Mechanical & Nuclear Engineering, University of Sharjah, 27272, Sharjah, UAE

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ASTM A106 B grade is used to manufacture seamless steel pipe, which can be used at high temperature, pressure, and hard environmental conditions for oil and gas supplies but suffers from corrosion. Polyurea is an organic coating that is resistant to moisture, UV light, and chemicals and has a high range of service temperatures. In this study, the effect of polyurea coating with the addition of copper nanoparticles was investigated for improvement of anti-corrosion and mechanical behavior in 3.5 % NaCl solution for A106 steel. Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed to examine anticorrosion properties, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was used to examine quality coating, thickness, and distribution of copper nanoparticles. Wear, hardness, and tensile tests were performed to check mechanical properties. The corrosion rate decreases from 0.16264 mpy to 0.01376 mpy while coating resistance increases from 2.95 × 104 Ω cm2 to 2.474 × 105 Ω cm2. An increasing trend was observed during the tensile test for pure polyurea and pure polyurea with 2 % copper nanoparticles. i.e., 501 MPa and 536 MPa, respectively. Results showed pure polyurea and polyurea with copper nanoparticles on steel improve its anti-corrosion and mechanical properties significantly.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is a global issue for which everyone must find a solution [1]. The petroleum industry faces corrosion along with the accompanying corroding substances and mitigation strategies but it must be noted that no material is a panacea for all corrosion and its sub-effect challenges [2,3].

Comparing the polyurea-coated steel's anticorrosion effectiveness to that of anticorrosive black paints, the hydrophobicity of the surface would be increased by the polyurea coatings on railway passenger coaches, [4]. Because offshore structures are often vulnerable to corrosion [5,6] fouling, fatigue, and other damage mechanisms, protection techniques are essential to extending a structure‘s intended service life [7]. Studies on the causes underlying coating system deterioration and advancements in the creation of binders and pigments may lead to the creation of anticorrosive coating systems with superior corrosion resistance [8]. In contrast to the separate situation, where the subsea pipeline can only function with 40 to 50 % of its original strength under the circumstance of combined failure modes, the combined failure probability of the two failure modes increases the failure risks [9]. Six times higher corrosion resistance is provided by polyurea than by the enamel coating now in use in industries [10]. Membranes, linings, and caulk are made of polyurea (PU), a copolymer created by the quick reaction of an isocyanate, and an amine component also by the increase in thickness of polyurea coatings, mechanical properties, of PU increase gradually [11]. Polyurea is used in industries, including building waterproofing, vehicle wear resistance, and beyond that of conventional coatings [12].

The periodic table being a basic tool for knowing element properties and place of element in specific group or period represent some specific trends in its behavior [13], Silver, belongs to group 11 or 1B in the periodic table has outstanding resistive properties [14,15]. The potential-Ph equilibrium or pourbaix diagram of Silver water system displayed silver stability in the water or aqueous media for all pH range [16,17].

Moreover, the predictable trends exhibited by metal oxides within the periodic table further aid in anticipating their properties [18]. Copper, situated directly above silver in group 11 possess similar behavior [19] like anti corrosive properties especially when used as nanoparticles [20] Copper cost less than Silver which make it better choice for different application [21] and availability of nanoparticles in required size ranges made a more practical [22].

Pourbaix diagram of copper-water system showed copper (
E
∘
= 0.337 V )
is more noble and better water stability than Iron [23]. Literature reported the efficiency of copper oxides in providing better corrosion resistance [24–26] and nanoparticles effectiveness as anti-corrosive agent [27]. The correlation between element place in periodic table and its intrinsic properties along with behavior in different corrosive media play a vital role in practical applications of material science and Engineering.

Copper and its alloys are used in a variety of industrial manufacturing processes as building materials for transportation, built-up machinery, and power transmission and generation [28], an alternative to silver and gold, copper can be used because of its potential value [29], and high corrosion resistance [30]. The copper nanoparticles inhibit corrosion by forming a protective layer over the substrate surface. In a coating, the nanoparticles create a coherent layer that acts as a barrier to the diffusive agents of corrosion. For instance, moisture and ions are prevented from reaching the underlying material. This action reduces the rate of corrosion by effectively slowing the process of degradation. Additionally, the copper nanoparticles may release copper to the exposed space, which may further inhibit corrosion by reacting with corrosive species [31]. The tested paints’ ability to suppress corrosion rate in the order of 0.5 percent Cu-NPs, 1 percent Cu-NPs, 2 percent Cu-NPs, 4 percent Cu-NPs, and 0 percent Cu-NPs [31]. When copper nanoparticles are included in the coatings, the corrosion current density is reduced. By utilizing copper nanoparticles, more cathodic reactions can occur on the surface. The increased occurrence of cathodic reactions consumes free electrons available and negates the anodic corrosion reaction. Consequently, the total corrosion current density reduces, which exhibits a lower rate of corrosion process [32].

In this work, Copper nanoparticles are incorporated into polyurethane to evaluate its effect on anti-corrosive and mechanical property of mild steel (ASTM 106) which is not reported in literature. Metallic Nanoparticles having small size are preferable due to high surface area to volume ratio.

Seamless pipe of mild steel ASTM 106 grade b is purchased from Huffaz Seamless Pipes Industries Ltd, having a nominal diameter of 42.2 mm and 2 feet in length, thickness of 3.56 mm. Chemical composition is given in Table 1 . 2 kg EPAPROOF FCA 930 Polyurea with two components (A + B) was purchased from EPAFLEX POLYURETHANES company. EP-ZR-70 Zinc Rich Epoxy primer was purchased from Andrew Paint. A 5 g pack of Copper Nano Particles having a mesh size of 25 nm was purchased by Sigma Aldrich United States. Table 2 shows the different properties of copper nanoparticles.

Mild steel ASTM 106 grade B was added into the jaw for sandblasting. For abrasive blasting, a blasting agent of Aluminum oxide grit size 150 and angular shape was used. Abrasive blasting was done according to ISO 8502-3:2017 standards. After the clean surface cut 10 mm width size rings from pipe and 10 mm × 10 mm pieces for EIS, PDP, SEM, and Hardener testing. For tensile testing according to ASTM E8 dog bone shape was cut by EDM wire cut with BM-4 protective lubricating fluid, and a wear sample according to G99-17 was prepared. EP-ZR-70 Zinc Rich Epoxy as a primer was used. Since the B component has less viscosity some calculated amount of copper nanoparticles suspension was prepared by sonicator mix followed by missing A component and stir for 15 min. All the sample coating was done by immersion either pure polyurea or with the addition of copper nanoparticles.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests were done by Potentiostat Interface 1000-E of Gamry Instruments USA. Both test was done according to the standards of ASTM G106 and ASTM G61 respectively. Potentiostat interface 1000-E of Gamry instruments is designed for a broad variety of different fields.

For electrochemical values evaluation samples coated with zero percent polyurea and 2 % copper nanoparticles are added by weight percentage and make two samples S1 and S2 respectively. The sample size was 10 mm × 10 mm and three electrodes were used one as standard electrode and two are electrochemical cells S1 and S2. Platinum wire as auxiliary electrode and reference electrode as calomel standard electrode in 3.5 % solution of sodium chloride working as system electrolyte. This experiment was performed by Potentiostat Interface 1000-E of Gamry Instruments USA. 100 kHz to 10 MHz was the frequency range with AC perturbation (RMS) 10 mV. Fig. 1 , R1 shows the resistance of solution, R2 coating resistance, and Q1 constant phase element (CPE) at coating [33] to evaluate the Randle circuit for fitting of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Fig. 1 shows the fitting circuit.

To test a material (ASTM A106 grade B) hardness, normally press an object an “indenter” into its surface that has been precisely loaded and dimensioned. Measurements of the indenter's penetration depth or the size of the indenter impression are used to assess the hardness (Hardness Rockwell B) HRB was performed according to ASTM E18.

Pin-on-disc apparatus is used to calculate wear, coefficient of friction, Frictional force, and wear rate. Pin-on-disc apparatus gives a wide range of parameters (Time, Load, Heating effect, RPM, and Lubrication) Koehler Instruments Company Pin-on Disc Apparatus is used in this study. In this 3 mm and 8 mm diameter samples can be used also 8 mm × 8 mm square samples of average height 25 mm samples can be used in this apparatus. Wear measurement was done according to ASTM G99-17.

UTM is used to find the tensile strength, Yield strength, compressive strength, percentage elongation, and reduction of percentage Area, “Universal” depicted in the name, it is versatile for the many materials. In this study, UTM TT-SHI-3000 is used for ASTM E18 for testing.

The sample's surface topography, crystal structure, material orientation, and morphology are revealed by the signals that are created because of the electrons' interactions with the sample's atoms. In this study, SEM was used for the structure observation of base metal ASTM A106 grade B, Zinc Rich Epoxy, and Polyurea. In this study, TESCAN Company VEGA3 SEM was used.

At the material surface, the resultant of the corrosion reactions scales within the current density. When copper nanoparticles are included in the coatings, the corrosion current density is reduced. By utilizing copper nanoparticles, more cathodic reactions can occur on the surface. The increased occurrence of cathodic reactions consumes free electrons available and negates the anodic corrosion reaction. Consequently, the total corrosion current density reduces, which exhibits a lower rate of corrosion process [32]. Corrosion current density and corrosion potential were calculated by extraction of the Tafel plot in Origin Pro software. Table 3 shows Tafel's extraction of data from Tafel plots. It shows that by applying copper nanoparticles corrosion current density is improved by 118 times. In a previous study, it was only 5.9 times [34]. The reason is that in the previous study ASTM A194 steel, E300 grade polyurea, silver nanoparticles, and no primer were used but here ASTM A106 steel pipe, finish polyurea, copper nanoparticles, and zinc-rich primer are used. In Table 3 R p is calculated with the help of Eq. (1). In this equation b a and b c are anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes respectively, R p is the corrosion resistance, and, (Icorr ) is corrosion current density.
(1)
R p
=
b c
b a
(
b c
+
b a
)
I
c o r r
From Table 3 it is concluded that by the decrease in corrosion current density corrosion resistance increases.

Table 4 shows (Icorr ) is corrosion current density, (Ecorr ) is corrosion potential, and CR corrosion rate in mile per year. The values of corrosion current density for the S1 sample of pure polyurea and polyurea with 2 % copper nanoparticles S2 are 1.761165 and 0.014897. According to improved corrosion, potential is established so (Ecorr ) is improved from −0.95963 to −0.70043. The corrosion rate improves by 11.8 times from polyurea with 2 % copper nanoparticles to pure polyurea. The corrosion rate is calculated with the help of Eq. (2). In this CR, is the corrosion rate in mile per year (Icorr ) is the corrosion current density in microampere per centimeter square, Ew equivalent weight/Atomic weight of iron in a gram of base sample, and A is area of sample in centimeter square. Patina is naturally active coating appeared on steel when exposed to corrosive environment especially humid and high chlorine regions [35]. But corrosion resistance of such coating decreases due to repeated exposure to extreme weather conditions. Polymer coatings such as polyurea, polyurethane, epoxy and black enamel coating are applied for better adhesion and corrosion protection [36]. Among polymeric coatings, polyurea gives 1.51 times less corrosion rate than anti-corrosive Black in 3.5 % NaCl solution because of increase of hydrophobicity which lowers the interaction of metal or substrate with electrolyte that improves corrosion resistance [4]. Also, polyurea shows 5.92 times less corrosion rate than black enamel coating [10]. This similar trend is seen in sample S1 which is pure polyurea and better corrosion resistance is observed by the addition copper nanoparticle in polyurea.
(2)
C R = 0.13 ×
E
c o r r
×
E w
d × A

The electrochemical data was analyzed and fitted to a Randle circuit model using Echem Analyst and Gamry Resonator eQCM 10 M software. Table 5 shows the result of the Randle circuit solution, The R1 solution resistance of S1 is 3.884 Ω cm2 and S2 has 72.71 Ω cm2 which increases 18.7 times. Q1 is a constant phase element (CPE) for coating its value for S1 is 1.00 × 10−8 and for S2 is 3.073 × 10−8. Film resistance or coating resistance (R2) of S1 is 2.95 × 104 and for S2 2.474 × 105 which means that S2 is 8.38 times more resistant. The S2 sample contains 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles, which means that by the addition of copper nanoparticles anti-corrosion properties are enhanced and improved.

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) tests were conducted in 3.5 wt.% NaCl to explain the electrochemical behavior of polyurea coated (S1) and copper nanoparticle infused polyurea coated (S2) samples. Fig. 3 (a) and (b) shows the Nyquist plot a and b of the S1 sample and S2 sample respectively. The Nyquist graph horizontal axis (x-axis) shows the real part of electrode impedance which is anode impedance. The anode capacitive reactance and inductive reactance reflects by vertical axis which is labeled as imaginary part of impedance (Zim). The coating Nyquist Plots are composed of high frequency capacitor loop and large arc radius. The high frequency capacitor is governed by corrosion process of the anode or working electrode. The large arc radius indicates difficulty in charge transfer which results in improvement of corrosion resistance [37]. Fig 3(a) depicts quite large arc radius which indicates better corrosion resistance as compared to polyurea coated sample S1 curve (b). It is believed that improvement of corrosion protection in polyurea coated samples after adding copper nanoparticles is because of two factors or reasons. First, nanoparticles are altering the coating defects and restricting the mobility of harmful chemicals or species which can initiate corrosion. Second the chemical reactivity between nanoparticles in polyurea coating and corrosion species [33]. High value of R2 in Table 5 confirms the enhancement of corrosion behavior due to addition of copper nanoparticles in polyurea coating sample. These nanoparticles delay the flow of corrosive nature ions from electrolyte to the mild steel through coating by filling the defects and stopping the easy path [33]. These nanoparticles may transform the structure and properties of the coating which may include adhesiveness, barrier properties, or improved chemical resistance. Such changes can be seen as differentiation in the electrochemical behavior of coated materials, which may cause changes in impedance values. Nanoparticles may also affect the coating's capacitance, Q1 of the Randles circuit model. A change in the composition and structure of the coating that results from nanoparticle incorporation may alter the electrical characteristics, which may result in differences in measured values of capacitance between the two samples. Overall, the range gap between the Nyquist plots of the sample with pure polyurea and the sample with 2 % nanoparticles reflects the differences in their electrochemical behavior and highlights the enhanced corrosion protection provided by the nanoparticle-modified coating.

Copper nanoparticles can increase the hardness of the coating by reinforcing the polymer matrix. If copper nanoparticles are regularly distributed across the coating, they enable the matrix to make the material tougher and prevent it from deforming and wearing. This quality is vital in situations where the coating is subjected to mechanical stressors [38]. Table 6 shows the three samples and their Rockwell hardness scale values. It is concluded that the hardness of the bare steel sample of A106 steel is 77.4 HRB, Hardness with pure polyurea coating is 85.8, and, polyurea with 2 % nanoparticles has 103.4HRB

Table 7 shows three samples of bare steel ASTM A106, steel with pure polyurea (S1) and S2 polyurea with 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles. It can be seen that Yield strength, Tensile Strength, and Percentage Elongation improved by 1.39, 1.2, and 1.3 times respectively as compared to pure polyurea with no additives also Yield strength, Tensile Strength, and Percentage Elongation of polyurea with 2 % nanoparticles improves 1.08, 1.06 and 1.04 times from polyurea with no additives.

Pin-on disc apparatus is used to observe the wearing behavior of pure polyurea and polyurea with 2 wt.% nanoparticles of copper metal. ASTM G99-17 standard is followed to perform wear tests. 400RPM, 2000 cycles, 300 s, 25 mm track radius, 25 °C temperature, Air atmosphere, and 19.5 N normal load were set as basic conditions to perform Pin on Disc wear test. Table 8 shows the related date of the wear test From Table 8, it can be concluded that copper nanoparticles make positive changes in polyurea and make it harder and more resistant to wear. Sample S1 has 62 micrometer material wear value in 300 s while Sample S2 has 46 micrometers. It reveals that by adding copper nanoparticles polyurea becomes harder and more resistant to wear. The wear rate of S1 is 0.0004969 mm3/Nm while S2 has 0.0002272 mm3/Nm which is 2.18 times that of S1. It can be concluded that by adding 2 % copper nanoparticles wear rate improves which is the best parameter to find the properties of polyurea for environmental and resistance to scratch and friction [40,41].

Different images are taken from different zooming values to see a clear structure of the layer. Fig. 4 (a) shows that base ASTM A106 metal has Zinc Rich Epoxy Primer and Polyurea with zero percent copper nanoparticles.

Samples coating was done on curvy-shaped metal pieces of 10mm × 10 mm. Fig. 4(b) shows the curvy shape of the sample. This was only done to restraining the surface morphology in the real state. At every moment for different processes, it was the priority to keep the surface in its original state. Also, shows the average thickness layer of polyurea which is 0.2 mm is 2 µm. Zinc-rich primers contain almost 90 % zinc which is conductive.
Fig. 4(c) shows the SEM image of zinc-rich primer and primer is used for its rough surface and creates void and ups and down surface to allow coating for high bonding and hence good adhesion.
Fig. 4(d) shows the polyurea image at 5 µm magnification. It can be observed that there are voids, air bubbles, and cracks, and no other defects found on the surface of the polyurea.

Fig. 4(e) shows the SEM image of 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles at 10 µm magnification. In this image it can be clearly shown that copper nanoparticles are present at the surface these are well distributed. Fig. 5 is another micrograph of S2 sample showing cracks, air voids but clusters of copper nanoparticles are not found, which means that copper nanoparticles are well mixed homogenously that matched with reported literature [42,43].
In this study, the protective and physical behavior of ASTM A106 grade B material was experimentally investigated.

By applying Polyurea coating in pure form and with 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles on ASTM A106 grade B corrosion current density decreases 1.761165 µAcm−2 to 0.014897 µAcm−2 while the electrode potential increase −0.95963 mV to −0.70043 mV also corrosion resistance increases 6.86 × 106 Ω to 6.32 × 109 Ω and corrosion rate decreases 0.16264 mpy to 0.01376 mpy. It means that corrosion current density improved 118 times, corrosion resistance improved, and corrosion rate improved 11.8 times. Copper nanoparticles influence the coating structure by minimizing defects and mobility of corrosion species. If any damage occur within coating, base metal is still protected because copper nanoparticles actively interact with chlorides and form compounds which settle down. Results of EIS showed that the value of R1 increased by 3.884 Ω cm2 to 72.71 Ω cm2 and R2 increased by 2.95 Ω cm2 × 104 to 2.474 × 105 Ω cm2 which means that solution resistance improved by 18.7 times and coating resistance improves 8.38 times. By analyzing the hardness result of samples HRB values were 77.4, 85.8, and 103.4 for bare steel, pure polyuria, and polyurea with copper nanoparticles respectively, it can be concluded that hardness improves 1.1 times from bare steel to pure polyurea and 1.33 times from pure polyurea to polyurea with 2 % copper nanoparticles. The wear rate of pure polyurea was 0.0004969 mm3/Nm and polyurea with 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles was 0.0002272 mm3/Nm. It can be concluded that the wear rate improved 2.18 times. Yield strengths were 240 MPa, 334 MPa and 364 MPa and Tensile strengths were 415 Mpa, 501 Mpa, and 536 Mpa of bare steel, pure polyurea, and polyurea with 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles respectively. It can be concluded that yield strength and tensile strength improved 1.39 times and 1.2 times respectively from bare steel to samples coated with pure polyurea. Also, yield strength and tensile strength improved by 1.08 and 1.06 times respectively when compared with pure polyurea and polyurea with 2 wt.% copper nanoparticles. SEM analysis showed that an average 200 µm thin layer of polyurea, sample has three parts, that is base metal ASTM A106 steel, Zinc rich primer, and polyurea. From the images, it can be concluded that copper nanoparticles were homogeneously mixed in polyurea, and no clusters were formed which provide better anti corrosive and mechanical properties.

Zeeshan Ahmed Siddiqui: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Aneela Wakeel: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Muhammad Ali Nasir: Supervision, Investigation. Muhammad Zubair: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Muhammad Ammar: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The paper accurately and thoroughly reflects the authors' research and analysis.
